content.text: Capacity management without a review cadence produces capacity decisions made in response to incidents rather than in anticipation of demand. A team that only reviews GPU allocation when P95 TTFT breaches the SLO is always reacting; a team with a structured weekly, monthly, quarterly, and annual review cycle identifies shortfalls before they manifest as SLO violations and makes infrastructure investments in planned procurement cycles rather than emergency spot-market purchases.

A capacity review cadence is a structured schedule of recurring meetings, with defined agendas, participants, data prerequisites, and decision outputs, that governs how a team monitors current infrastructure utilisation, validates demand forecasts, adjusts autoscaling policies, and plans future procurement. Each review cadence layer has a different time horizon: weekly reviews address operational adjustments, monthly reviews update the 30-day forecast and scaling policy, quarterly reviews produce the 90-day capacity plan and budget reconciliation, and the annual review produces the 12-month infrastructure commitment.

For agentic AI systems, the review cadence must include AI-specific metrics and decision points that standard infrastructure capacity reviews omit: token volume and compute-unit trends (not just request count), forecast accuracy against the demand model, LLM provider reserved capacity utilisation, and the impact of planned model changes (new model versions, context window changes, new tool integrations) on the capacity plan.

The cadence produces documented outputs at each cycle: meeting notes with action items, updated forecast documents, scaling policy PRs, and quarterly plan documents. These outputs are the mechanism by which capacity decisions are communicated to finance, product, and engineering leadership, and by which accountability for action items is maintained between reviews.

Without a structured review cadence, capacity management degrades into reactive fire-fighting. The pattern is predictable: the team adds capacity after each SLO violation, over-provisions to create a safety buffer, and the safety buffer erodes as traffic grows without triggering another response because the buffer absorbs the growth. Eventually, the buffer is exhausted and the next traffic spike causes a violation worse than the one that prompted the original over-provisioning.

The cadence breaks this cycle by making capacity a regular agenda item rather than an emergency topic. A team that reviews GPU utilisation every week catches a gradual utilisation creep from 60% to 72% over six weeks and initiates a controlled capacity increase before the creep reaches 85% and causes violations. A team that reviews only when alerted misses the gradual creep entirely.

For regulated industries and enterprise customers, the capacity review cadence is also a governance mechanism. Enterprise SLAs require evidence that the service provider performs proactive capacity planning. The capacity review outputs — plan documents, action items, budget reconciliations — constitute this evidence. An annual procurement cycle for reserved GPU capacity cannot be executed without a quarterly planning document that justifies the commitment level.
Regular review of demand forecasts and utilisation trends identifies capacity shortfalls 30-90 days before they manifest as SLO violations.
Quarterly budget reconciliation catches cost overruns early and provides the data needed to revise the budget before the overrun compounds.
Monthly review of autoscale trigger frequency identifies oscillation, threshold miscalibration, and pre-scaling failures before they accumulate into patterns of SLO violations.

Quarterly review of LLM vendor contract utilisation and annual review of reserved capacity commitment levels prevent both over-commitment (paying for unused capacity) and under-commitment (losing volume discounts).

Involving finance and product in monthly and quarterly reviews ensures that capacity decisions reflect business context — planned product launches, marketing campaigns, user growth projections — not just historical trends.
A well-run weekly review takes 30 minutes; a monthly review takes 90 minutes; a quarterly review takes a half-day. This is non-trivial meeting load for engineering teams under delivery pressure.
Without discipline, capacity reviews become status updates rather than decision-making sessions. The agenda must be structured to force decisions, not just data presentation.
A review that produces action items that are not tracked or completed is worse than no review — it creates the impression of capacity management without the substance.

Monthly reviews are based on data that is 1-4 weeks old. Major traffic changes (a product launch, a viral event) that happen after the last review and before the next are not addressed until the following cycle unless the team has mechanisms to identify and act on extraordinary events.

The capacity review cadence operates at four time scales, each with a different focus and different participants. The weekly review is operational: the SRE on-call engineer reviews the SLO burn rate, forecast MAPE, and autoscaling event frequency. If any metric is out of range, a ticket is created and assigned before the next weekly review.

The monthly review is tactical: the SRE lead, ML engineering lead, finance partner, and product PM review the 30-day demand forecast update, error budget consumption, token volume growth rate, and reserved capacity utilisation. The meeting produces a scaling policy update (if needed), a cost forecast for the next 30 days, and a list of action items with owners.

The quarterly review is strategic: the full capacity plan for the next 90 days is reviewed, the budget is reconciled against actuals, LLM vendor contracts are reviewed for utilisation and upcoming renewal, and the capacity implications of roadmap items for the next quarter are incorporated into the plan. The meeting produces a formal capacity plan document that is signed off by engineering and finance leadership.

The annual review is a planning exercise that produces the 12-month infrastructure budget, the reserved instance commitment level for the following year, and the architecture evolution plan that will change the capacity profile of the system over the year.

attrs.alt: Capacity review cadence: four-tier weekly/monthly/quarterly/annual cycle with defined outputs at each level

content.text: The four review tiers have different time horizons, participants, and outputs. Weekly reviews focus on operational metrics and produce tactical adjustments. Monthly reviews produce updated forecasts and scaling policy changes. Quarterly reviews produce the 90-day plan and budget reconciliation. Annual reviews produce the 12-month budget commitment.

A structured capacity review cadence is appropriate for any agentic AI system with meaningful infrastructure costs (above $5,000/month) or with SLAs that make SLO violations commercially significant. Below this threshold, informal weekly check-ins and quarterly budget reviews are sufficient.

The specific cadence structure should match the team size and maturity. A small team (3-5 engineers) can consolidate the weekly and monthly reviews into a single bi-weekly 45-minute review. A large platform team serving multiple products needs the full four-tier cadence to keep stakeholders aligned across the different time horizons.

The cadence should be initiated before the system reaches production scale, not after the first capacity incident. Establishing the review structure during the ramp-to-production phase means the team already has established reporting, data pipelines, and meeting rhythms when the system is under load.

# docs/capacity-review-agenda.md (template)

## Monthly Capacity Review Agenda

**Attendees:** SRE lead, ML engineering lead, finance partner, product PM
**Duration:** 90 minutes
**Prerequisites:** Dashboard snapshot sent 24h before meeting

---

### 1. Metrics review (20 min)

| Metric | Last Month | Trend | Target |
|--------|------------|-------|--------|
| P95 TTFT (ms) | | | < 2000 |
| Error budget consumed | | | < 50% |
| Total token volume (B) | | | Forecast ± 15% |
| GPU utilisation avg | | | 55-75% |
| Cost per 1M output tokens | | | Budget ± 10% |

### 2. Forecast accuracy review (15 min)

- MAPE for last 30 days (target: < 15%)
- If MAPE > 15%: root cause (distribution shift / feature launch / data gap)
- Model retraining decision: YES / NO

### 3. Demand forecast for next 30 days (20 min)

- P50 forecast: __ GPU-hours
- P95 forecast: __ GPU-hours
- Cost projection at P50: $__ / P95: $__
- Key events affecting next 30 days: (list feature launches, campaigns)

### 4. Scaling policy review (20 min)

- Review autoscale trigger frequency: __ scale-out events, __ scale-in events
- Identify oscillation (scale-out followed by scale-in within 30 min): YES / NO
- Threshold adjustment proposal: (none / increase / decrease)

### 5. Action items from last month (10 min)

- Review open action items
- Close completed actions
- Carry over incomplete actions with updated owner and due date

### 6. New action items (5 min)

- Document: owner, due date, success criterion

# monitoring/capacity-dashboard.py
"""
Generates the capacity review dashboard data package sent to reviewers
24 hours before each monthly capacity review.
"""
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
import pandas as pd
import boto3, json

METRICS_BUCKET = "ops-data-lake"
REPORT_BUCKET  = "ops-reports"

def fetch_monthly_metrics(month_ago: datetime) -> dict:
    client = boto3.client("athena")
    query = """
    SELECT
        date_trunc('day', timestamp) as day,
        avg(p95_ttft_ms) as avg_p95_ttft,
        sum(input_tokens) / 1e9 as input_tokens_b,
        sum(output_tokens) / 1e9 as output_tokens_b,
        avg(gpu_utilisation_pct) as avg_gpu_util,
        sum(cost_usd) as total_cost_usd
    FROM genai_metrics
    WHERE timestamp >= date_add('month', -1, now())
    GROUP BY 1
    ORDER BY 1
    """
    # ... execute query and return results
    return {}

def compute_error_budget_consumed(slo_window_days=30, slo_target=0.999) -> float:
    # Error budget = 1 - SLO target = 0.1% = 43.2 min per 30 days
    # Consumed = actual downtime / error budget
    allowed_downtime_minutes = (1 - slo_target) * slo_window_days * 24 * 60
    # Fetch actual downtime from incident records
    actual_downtime_minutes = 12.0  # example
    return actual_downtime_minutes / allowed_downtime_minutes

def generate_report(month_start: datetime) -> dict:
    metrics = fetch_monthly_metrics(month_start)
    error_budget = compute_error_budget_consumed()
    return {
        "report_period": month_start.strftime("%Y-%m"),
        "generated_at": datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        "error_budget_consumed_pct": round(error_budget * 100, 1),
        "metrics_summary": metrics,
        "next_review_date": (month_start + timedelta(weeks=4)).strftime("%Y-%m-%d"),
    }

# capacity/quarterly_plan.py
"""
Generates the 90-day capacity plan document from the demand forecast
and current reserved capacity state.
"""
from dataclasses import dataclass
import yaml, json
from pathlib import Path

@dataclass
class QuarterlyCapacityPlan:
    quarter: str
    p50_gpu_hours: float
    p95_gpu_hours: float
    current_reserved_gpu_hours: float
    shortfall_gpu_hours: float
    cost_projection_usd: float
    recommendations: list[str]

    def to_markdown(self) -> str:
        lines = [
            f"# Quarterly Capacity Plan: {self.quarter}",
            "",
            "## Summary",
            f"- P50 demand forecast: {self.p50_gpu_hours:,.0f} GPU-hours",
            f"- P95 demand forecast: {self.p95_gpu_hours:,.0f} GPU-hours",
            f"- Current reserved capacity: {self.current_reserved_gpu_hours:,.0f} GPU-hours",
            f"- Shortfall at P95: {max(0, self.shortfall_gpu_hours):,.0f} GPU-hours",
            f"- Cost projection (P50): ${self.cost_projection_usd:,.0f}",
            "",
            "## Recommendations",
        ]
        for i, rec in enumerate(self.recommendations, 1):
            lines.append(f"{i}. {rec}")
        return "\n".join(lines)

def build_plan(forecast_json: str, reserved_yaml: str) -> QuarterlyCapacityPlan:
    forecast = json.loads(Path(forecast_json).read_text())
    reserved = yaml.safe_load(Path(reserved_yaml).read_text())

    p50 = forecast["p50_gpu_hours_90d"]
    p95 = forecast["p95_gpu_hours_90d"]
    current = reserved["total_reserved_gpu_hours"]
    shortfall = p95 - current

    recommendations = []
    if shortfall > 0:
        recommendations.append(
            f"Increase reserved capacity by {shortfall:,.0f} GPU-hours before quarter start"
        )
    if forecast["mape_last_30d"] > 0.15:
        recommendations.append(
            "Retrain demand forecast model — MAPE exceeds 15% threshold"
        )
    if reserved["utilisation_pct"] < 0.55:
        recommendations.append(
            f"Reserved capacity utilisation {reserved['utilisation_pct']:.0%} is below 55% — consider releasing {current * 0.1:,.0f} GPU-hours"
        )

    return QuarterlyCapacityPlan(
        quarter=forecast["quarter"],
        p50_gpu_hours=p50,
        p95_gpu_hours=p95,
        current_reserved_gpu_hours=current,
        shortfall_gpu_hours=shortfall,
        cost_projection_usd=p50 * 3.50,
        recommendations=recommendations,
    )
Alert rules to trigger ad-hoc reviews

# monitoring/capacity-alerts.yaml
# Prometheus alerting rules for capacity review triggers

groups:
  - name: capacity-review-triggers
    rules:
      - alert: CapacityForecastMAPEHigh
        expr: genai_forecast_mape_7d > 0.15
        for: 1d
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: sre
        annotations:
          summary: "Demand forecast MAPE exceeds 15% — retrain before next capacity review"
          runbook: "https://wiki/runbooks/capacity/forecast-mape-high"

      - alert: GPUUtilisationAboveTarget
        expr: avg(genai_gpu_utilisation_pct) > 75
        for: 4h
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: sre
        annotations:
          summary: "GPU utilisation above 75% target for 4h — consider proactive scale-out"

      - alert: ReservedCapacityShortfall
        expr: genai_p95_forecast_gpu_hours_30d > genai_reserved_gpu_hours * 0.95
        for: 1d
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: sre
        annotations:
          summary: "P95 forecast exceeds 95% of reserved capacity — increase reservation"

      - alert: ErrorBudgetHalfConsumed
        expr: genai_error_budget_consumed_pct > 50
        for: 1h
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: sre
        annotations:
          summary: "Error budget 50% consumed — freeze non-critical changes, review capacity"

Weekly review for a two-person SRE team: a 20-minute Monday standup reviewing four metrics (P95 TTFT, GPU utilisation, cost trend, forecast MAPE). Any metric out of range creates a Jira ticket with a due date before the next standup. The review is async-first: metrics are checked in a shared dashboard and the standup only happens if a ticket was created.

Quarterly review for an enterprise AI platform team: a half-day session with SRE lead, ML engineering lead, VP Engineering, finance director, and product director. The morning covers metrics review, forecast accuracy assessment, and current scaling policy evaluation. The afternoon covers the 90-day capacity plan, budget reconciliation, vendor contract review, and roadmap capacity impact assessment. The session produces a signed capacity plan document and an updated reserved capacity commitment.

A media company operating an AI content moderation agent for user-generated content established a monthly capacity review cadence in the first quarter of operating the system. The initial setup took 3 months: the first month to establish the metrics dashboard, the second month to build the demand forecast model, and the third month to run the first full monthly review.

In month 4, the monthly review identified that the forecast MAPE for video content traffic was 28% (well above the 15% target) due to unpredictable viral content upload events. The review produced two action items: first, build a real-time traffic anomaly detector that triggers an ad-hoc capacity review whenever traffic deviates more than 30% from the 15-minute-ahead forecast; second, negotiate a burst capacity agreement with the cloud provider for on-demand GPU instances with a 3-minute provisioning SLA.

Both action items were completed within 6 weeks. The burst capacity agreement cost 15% more per GPU-hour than reserved instances but eliminated the unplanned cost spikes that had appeared in the monthly budget reconciliation. The anomaly detector triggered two ad-hoc capacity reviews in the next quarter, both of which identified and resolved capacity shortfalls before they caused SLO violations.
A capacity review that presents data but produces no decisions, adjustments, or action items is a waste of time. Every review must produce at least one documented output.
Holding a capacity review without sending the dashboard snapshot and key metrics 24 hours in advance means the meeting time is spent interpreting data rather than making decisions.

Action items produced in a review that are not tracked in a persistent system (Jira, linear, etc.) are frequently incomplete by the next review, eroding the team's confidence that the review process produces real outcomes.
Finance not involved in quarterly reviews:

Capacity planning decisions have budget implications. A quarterly review that does not include a finance partner produces capacity plans that conflict with budget constraints and must be revised after the fact.
Annual review skipped due to delivery pressure:

The annual reserved capacity commitment determines the infrastructure cost floor for the next 12 months. Missing the annual review means the commitment is made without the benefit of the planning process.
The dashboard snapshot sent before each review must be generated automatically. Manual data preparation introduces errors and delays that erode review quality.

Document the metric thresholds that require action at each review cadence (e.g., MAPE > 15% triggers model retraining, utilisation > 75% triggers scaling policy review). This converts subjective judgment calls into objective decisions.
Make quarterly capacity plan documents available to all engineering and finance stakeholders, not just meeting attendees. Visibility creates accountability.

At the annual review, assess the quality of the review cadence itself: Did weekly reviews catch issues before they became incidents? Did quarterly plans match actuals? Improve the cadence based on evidence.
Separate operational reviews from planning reviews:

The weekly operational review and the quarterly planning review require different participants and produce different outputs. Merging them into a single meeting produces a session that is too long and unfocused.

The review cadence scales with team and system size by adjusting the depth of each tier, not the number of tiers. A small team might condense the monthly and quarterly reviews into a single bi-monthly session. A large team with multiple AI services might have service-level weekly and monthly reviews plus a platform-level quarterly review that aggregates across services.

The data preparation automation is the scalability bottleneck. If the capacity dashboard requires manual data extraction and formatting, it will not scale beyond a small team. The Athena/BigQuery queries that power the dashboard should run in under 5 minutes and produce a structured data package that is automatically formatted into the review template.
SQL aggregations for review data package
Links action items from review notes to tickets
Bot posts weekly metrics snapshot to SRE channel

Capacity review data contains commercially sensitive information: traffic volumes, cost figures, and LLM vendor contract terms. Access to capacity review documents and the metrics dashboards that feed them should be restricted to the SRE team, ML engineering, finance, and leadership — not exposed to all employees.
Restrict the capacity metrics dashboard to the capacity review participants and SRE leadership. The data reveals traffic patterns that could inform competitive intelligence.
Mark quarterly capacity plan documents as confidential. They contain budget figures and vendor contract information.
Retain capacity metrics for at least 24 months to support year-over-year trend analysis and audit requirements for regulated industries.
If the automated dashboard generation fails, the review should not be cancelled — maintain a manual fallback procedure for generating the key metrics from raw data.

A capacity review cadence is an early-warning system operating at multiple time scales simultaneously. The weekly review is a short-range radar that detects immediate obstacles (SLO burn rate spikes, autoscaling oscillation). The monthly review is a medium-range radar that detects approaching terrain (demand forecast updates, cost trends). The quarterly review is a long-range radar that detects horizon-level threats (seasonal peaks, product launches). The annual review is a navigation plan that sets the overall route.

Each radar has a different resolution and range. The short-range radar is precise about the immediate environment but cannot see far ahead. The long-range radar sees far ahead but has lower resolution. Together, they give the team a complete picture of the operational environment at every relevant time scale.

A four-tier cadence (weekly, monthly, quarterly, annual) provides coverage at operational, tactical, strategic, and commitment time scales — each tier focuses on a different horizon and involves different stakeholders.
Every review must produce documented outputs (action items, updated plans, policy PRs) and tracked action items with owners and due dates; a review that produces no decisions is a waste.
Automate data preparation so that the review dashboard snapshot is generated without manual effort — manual preparation introduces errors and delays that erode review quality.
Finance must be involved in monthly and quarterly reviews; capacity decisions without budget context produce plans that conflict with financial constraints.
Define metric decision thresholds in advance (MAPE > 15% triggers retraining, utilisation > 75% triggers policy review) to convert subjective judgment into objective actions.
The annual reserved capacity commitment determines infrastructure cost for 12 months; missing the annual review is a significant operational and financial risk.
